title: Friday Sermon 12 April 2002
description:
On the Divine attribute of al-Jabbar – which translates as The Subduer or The One who Subdues.  In my Urdu exegesis of The Holy Quran, I have explained al-Jabbar as One Who make amends.[1]

Al-Jabbar is that Being Who can compel mankind to praise and exalt Him.[2]  It has been derived from an Arabic phrase that denotes to favour and patronise a needy person, it also connotes to reform with domination and might.  When used for man, the word Jabbar has a censorious significance and the compulsion and coercion associated with the word adopts the elements of His all-encompassing Mercy and Grace when it is used for Allah.[3]

The Holy Prophet (peace and blessings be on him) said that the heart of man is as if between the two fingers of God the Subduer and He can, when He so wills, turn it:[4]

Asma bint Umais Al-Khathamiyyah narrated that The Messenger of Allah ﷺ said: What an evil servant is the one who fancies himself and becomes vain forgetting the Most Great, the Most High.  What an evil servant is the one who forces and behaves hostilely, forgetting the Compeller, the Most High.  What an evil servant is the one who is heedless and diverted, forgetting about the graves and the trials.  What an evil servant is the one who is violent and tyrannical, forgetting his beginnings or his end.  What an evil servant is the one who seeks the world by means of the faith.  What an evil servant is the one who seeks the religion through  his desires.  What an evil servant is the one who puts all hope in his own zeal.  What an evil servant is the worshipper who is misled by his desire.  What an evil servant is the one whose aspirations humiliate him.[5]

Another Hadith that recounts that on the Day of Judgment after all creation has had accountability and the two remaining persons are to be sent to hell, one of them will turn back and look.  God the Subduer will summon that person and ask as to why he had turned and looked.  The person will reply that he had hoped that he would be sent to heaven.  Upon this he will be sent to heaven.[6]

In the Quranic verses whereby the negative characteristic associated with the word Jabbar is mentioned it is in relation to those who opposed the prophets of God:

I myself saw a dervish, who on a very hot day would recite the verse: 'And when you lay hands you lay hands as tyrants';  and would seize hold of a wasp and be immune to its sting.  I have also experienced some wonderful effects of verses of 'The Holy Quran', which display the wonders of the Power of God, the Glorious.

(Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad.  'Surma Chashm Arya'.  Riyaz e Hind, 1886.  'Ruhani Khazain: ii.  100).

Based on my personal experience the reading of this verse protects one from harmful insect bites and stings.

[1] Ali al-Tabrisi says: the attribute of the subduer and the compeller when used for people is used in a negative context.  I do not agree to this because the compeller can also be used for those other than Allah as a term of praise, just as Hazrat Musleh Maud has stated the same.  The word compeller is used for the mending of a broken bone.  This word would be used as a form of praise for one who joins the bones.

(Hazrat Mirza Tahir Ahmad - Khalifaul Masih IV.  Darsul Quran, 15 December 1999).
[2] Ibn Manẓūr.  'Lisan al-Arab'.
[3] Abul Qasim al-Hussayn ibn Mufaddal ibn Muhammad al-Raghib. 'Al Mufradat fi Gharib al-Quran'.
[4] Ahmad ibn Hanbal.  'Musnad Ahmad ibn Hanbal'.
[5] Abu Isa Muhammad ibn Isa al-Tirmidhi.  'Jami al-Tirmidhi'.  
[6] Ibn Hanbal.
[7] 'The Holy Quran'.  Al Shuara [The Poets]: 131.
